**Macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin):**
Mechanism: bind 50S ribosomal subunit → inhibit protein synthesis.
- Excellent coverage of atypical pathogens (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydophila)
- QT prolongation risk: particularly azithromycin; monitor in high-risk patients
- GI upset (erythromycin is a motilin agonist → nausea, cramping, diarrhoea)
- Drug interactions: inhibit CYP3A4 (clarithromycin, erythromycin) → increased levels of statins, warfarin, digoxin

**Metronidazole:**
Mechanism: forms toxic metabolites by anaerobic bacteria that damage DNA.
- Used for anaerobic infections, CDI (oral), bacterial vaginosis, H. pylori (combo therapy)
- Disulfiram-like reaction: patient MUST avoid all alcohol during treatment and 48 hours after completion (even small amounts cause severe nausea, vomiting, flushing, tachycardia)
- Peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use
- Metallic taste — warn patients

**Clindamycin:**
Mechanism: binds 50S ribosome; bacteriostatic.
- Good coverage for Gram-positive and anaerobes; skin/soft tissue, dental infections, aspiration pneumonia
- Risk: C. difficile colitis — clindamycin is one of the highest-risk antibiotics for CDI
- Monitor for diarrhoea; test stool if ≥ 3 loose stools in 24h while on or after clindamycin

sections.heading: Adverse Effects and Allergy Recognition
sections.body:
**Antibiotic hypersensitivity — spectrum of reactions:**
- **Immediate hypersensitivity (Type I, IgE-mediated)**: occurs within minutes to 1 hour of first dose. Urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, hypotension, anaphylaxis. LIFE-THREATENING. Hold antibiotic, administer epinephrine IM, call emergency.
- **Accelerated urticaria**: 1–72 hours after dose; urticaria only; may continue with monitoring depending on severity.
- **Delayed reactions** (> 72 hours): maculopapular rash (most common antibiotic reaction overall — often NOT true allergy); may be drug-rash-eosinophilia-systemic symptoms (DRESS) — serious; Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare but severe).

**Red Man Syndrome (vancomycin) vs anaphylaxis:**
- Red Man: non-immunological histamine release; flushing, erythema, pruritus of upper body. No bronchospasm, no hypotension (usually). Treated by slowing infusion rate; not a true allergy.
- Anaphylaxis: IgE-mediated; bronchospasm, hypotension, urticaria. Life-threatening. Epinephrine immediately.

**C. difficile (antibiotic-associated colitis):**
- Watery, foul-smelling diarrhoea ≥ 3/day during or after antibiotic therapy
- Especially high risk: clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, broad-spectrum cephalosporins
- Do NOT administer anti-diarrhoeal agents (antiperistaltics → toxic megacolon)

**Antibiotic-specific toxicities to monitor:**
- Vancomycin: Red Man Syndrome, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity
- Aminoglycosides: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity (irreversible)
- Fluoroquinolones: tendon rupture, QT prolongation, peripheral neuropathy
- Tetracyclines: photosensitivity, dental/bone effects in children
- Metronidazole: disulfiram reaction with alcohol

sections.heading: Red Flags / Anaphylaxis and Sepsis
sections.body:
**Anaphylaxis — STOP antibiotic immediately, call emergency:**
- Urticaria/hives + bronchospasm and/or hypotension within 1 hour of antibiotic administration
- Administer epinephrine 1:1000, 0.3–0.5 mg IM (anterolateral thigh)
- Lay patient flat (Trendelenburg if no respiratory distress)
- High-flow O₂
- IV access, IV fluids
- Diphenhydramine and corticosteroids are adjuncts — epinephrine is primary
- Document true penicillin allergy in chart; notify provider; do NOT re-challenge

**Delay in first antibiotic dose in sepsis:**
- In sepsis, every hour of delay in antibiotic administration increases mortality by ~7%
- First antibiotic dose should be administered within 1–3 hours of sepsis recognition, ideally after cultures are collected but without waiting for results
- If patient has known or suspected penicillin allergy: consult provider before delaying — most penicillin 'allergies' are not true anaphylaxis; cross-reactivity with cephalosporins is < 2%; alternative agents are usually available

sections.heading: Diagnostics / Labs
sections.body:
**Before starting antibiotics:**
- Obtain cultures first (blood × 2, urine, wound, sputum as appropriate) — once antibiotics are started, culture sensitivity falls significantly
- Allergy history: what was the reaction? When did it occur? Has the patient tolerated the drug since?

**Drug level monitoring:**
- **Vancomycin**: trough level just before next dose (goal: 15–20 mcg/mL for serious infections) OR AUC/MIC pharmacokinetic monitoring (preferred in many centres)
- **Aminoglycosides**: peak (1 hour post-dose) and trough (before next dose) for traditional dosing; for extended-interval (once-daily), a single level drawn at a specified time (Hartford nomogram)

**Monitoring for toxicity:**
- BMP at baseline and every 2–3 days for vancomycin and aminoglycosides: creatinine, BUN, electrolytes
- CBC for prolonged courses: thrombocytopaenia possible with some beta-lactams, vancomycin
- LFTs for prolonged isoniazid, rifampin (TB therapy)
- QTc monitoring: baseline ECG and periodic for fluoroquinolones, azithromycin in high-risk patients

**Antibiotic stewardship support role:**
- When the clinical picture changes (patient improving): advocate for de-escalation (narrower spectrum) or switch to oral
- When cultures return: confirm antibiotic aligns with sensitivities
- Question prolonged broad-spectrum courses without reassessment

sections.heading: Nursing Responsibilities
sections.body:
**Before every antibiotic dose:**
1. Verify the 'Rights' (right drug, dose, route, time, patient)
2. Check allergy status: ask the patient at every new admission; document the REACTION, not just the drug name ('penicillin — anaphylaxis' is very different from 'penicillin — mild rash 20 years ago')
3. Confirm cultures were obtained before this course was started
4. Assess relevant labs: renal function for renally cleared antibiotics; drug levels if applicable

**During infusion:**
- Monitor for infusion reactions, particularly with first dose
- For vancomycin: slow infusion rate if patient develops flushing (Red Man Syndrome)
- For aminoglycosides: monitor renal function and hearing (report tinnitus or dizziness promptly)
- Time administration: TIMING MATTERS in sepsis — administer on schedule, do not hold without clinical indication

**Oral antibiotic administration notes:**
- Fluoroquinolones: advise patient NOT to take within 2 hours of antacids, calcium supplements, iron, dairy products (chelation impairs absorption)
- Metronidazole: absolutely avoid alcohol; explain the disulfiram-like reaction
- Tetracyclines: take on empty stomach if possible; avoid milk/dairy, antacids, iron within 2 hours; use sunscreen (photosensitivity)
- Rifampin: warn patient that urine, sweat, saliva, and tears will be orange — contact lenses will stain permanently

**Patient rights and consent:**
- Patients have the right to refuse antibiotics; educate on consequences; document refusal and provider notification
- If patient has allergy to prescribed antibiotic: HOLD and notify provider before administering

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.body:
- **Cultures before antibiotics** — collect cultures first, start antibiotics promptly after (do not delay antibiotics waiting for culture to grow — just ensure collection happened).
- **Red Man Syndrome vs anaphylaxis**: Red Man = slow infusion-related histamine release (not true allergy, manage by slowing rate). Anaphylaxis = bronchospasm + hypotension + urticaria → epinephrine immediately.
- **Penicillin allergy cross-reactivity with cephalosporins**: < 2% true cross-reactivity. Many patients labelled as penicillin-allergic have never had a reaction — allergy label deprives them of first-line agents. Detailed allergy history matters.
- **Clindamycin → highest CDI risk** (along with fluoroquinolones and third-gen cephalosporins). Any diarrhoea during or after clindamycin = test for CDI.
- **Aminoglycoside + vancomycin**: additive nephrotoxicity risk. Monitor renal function aggressively.
- **Metronidazole = no alcohol**: not just beer and wine — alcohol is in many mouthwashes, cough syrups, and hand sanitisers absorbed through skin; advise broadly.
- **Oral antibiotics for MRSA skin infections**: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) or doxycycline — NOT standard cephalosporins or amoxicillin (MRSA is resistant).

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.body:
**Complete the full course:**
- Never stop antibiotics early because you feel better — incompletely treated infections often relapse and can develop resistance
- Take at evenly spaced intervals — consistent blood levels optimize bactericidal effect
- Store correctly: some liquid antibiotics require refrigeration; check the label

**Drug-specific instructions:**
- Metronidazole: avoid ALL forms of alcohol (including mouthwash with alcohol content) during treatment and for 48 hours after completion
- Fluoroquinolones: avoid antacids, calcium, iron within 2 hours; use sunscreen; stop and call provider if you develop tendon pain or severe weakness
- Tetracyclines: avoid sun exposure; do not give to children < 8 or pregnant women
- Rifampin: your urine, sweat, and tears will turn orange — this is expected and harmless

**When to seek care while on antibiotics:**
- Rash, hives, or itching (possible allergy — call provider before next dose)
- Difficulty breathing, lip/tongue swelling (anaphylaxis — call 911)
- New diarrhoea, especially after antibiotic course (possible C. difficile)
- No improvement after 48–72 hours — the antibiotic may not be covering the organism

**Antibiotic resistance prevention:**
- Never demand antibiotics for viral illnesses (colds, flu, most sore throats)
- Never share antibiotics or use leftover antibiotics for a different illness
- Never take someone else's prescription

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.body:
- Cultures BEFORE antibiotics. Start antibiotics within 1–3 hours in sepsis.
- Always verify allergy — document the REACTION, not just the drug. Penicillin-cephalosporin cross-reactivity < 2%.
- Vancomycin: slow infusion (≥ 60 min) prevents Red Man Syndrome. Monitor troughs and creatinine.
- Aminoglycosides: narrow therapeutic index. Monitor peak/trough levels, creatinine, and hearing.
- Fluoroquinolones: QT prolongation, tendon rupture risk. No antacids within 2h. Not in children/pregnancy.
- Metronidazole: NO alcohol during treatment and 48h after. Orange urine = rifampin.
- Clindamycin/fluoroquinolones → highest CDI risk. Diarrhoea during antibiotic course → test for CDI.
- Antibiotic stewardship: right drug, right dose, right duration. De-escalate when cultures return.

preTest.question: A client has a documented penicillin allergy described as 'anaphylaxis in 2012.' The provider orders ceftriaxone (a cephalosporin) for community-acquired pneumonia. What should the nurse do?
options: Administer ceftriaxone — penicillin and cephalosporins are completely different drug classes with no cross-reactivity

Administer ceftriaxone — cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins is < 2% overall; however, given a history of anaphylaxis, the nurse should contact the provider to confirm the order is intentional and clarify whether allergy testing or alternative antibiotics were considered
Refuse to administer ceftriaxone — any penicillin allergy is an absolute contraindication to all cephalosporins
Give ceftriaxone and keep epinephrine at the bedside without notifying the provider
preTest.rationale: Cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins is low (< 2%) and mainly occurs with shared side chains, not the beta-lactam ring itself. However, a documented anaphylaxis (the most severe hypersensitivity) warrants provider verification before proceeding. The nurse should confirm the order is intentional, ensure the provider is aware of the anaphylaxis history, and have resuscitation equipment available. Simply administering without verification or refusing outright without contacting the provider are both incorrect responses.

preTest.question: The nurse is administering gentamicin (an aminoglycoside). Which findings should the nurse report to the provider immediately?
options: Tinnitus and new onset of dizziness after the third dose
Mild nausea 30 minutes after the infusion
Pruritus at the IV site
Serum creatinine 72 µmol/L (unchanged from baseline)
preTest.rationale: Tinnitus and dizziness are early signs of aminoglycoside ototoxicity — both cochlear (hearing loss, tinnitus) and vestibular (vertigo, ataxia) damage can occur. Ototoxicity from aminoglycosides can be irreversible. These symptoms must be reported immediately to the provider, who may discontinue or adjust the antibiotic. Mild GI effects and unchanged creatinine are not urgent concerns. Pruritus at the IV site may indicate irritation but is not a priority in this context.

postTest.question: A client receiving clindamycin for a dental abscess calls the nurse to report 4–5 loose watery stools in the past 24 hours. What is the nurse's priority?
options: Reassure the client — diarrhoea is a common GI side effect of clindamycin and will resolve
Administer loperamide (Imodium) to control the diarrhoea
Notify the provider; collect a stool sample for C. difficile testing; do NOT administer anti-diarrhoeals
Hold the clindamycin and administer a probiotic
postTest.rationale: Clindamycin is one of the highest-risk antibiotics for C. difficile colitis. New diarrhoea during or after clindamycin must be tested for CDI with a stool C. difficile toxin/PCR assay. Anti-peristaltic agents (loperamide) are contraindicated in CDI — they can precipitate toxic megacolon by trapping toxin and bacteria in the colon. The provider must be notified to determine whether to continue clindamycin or switch.

postTest.question: A client reports that he has been prescribed ciprofloxacin for a UTI and plans to take it with his morning calcium supplement and antacid. What is the nurse's teaching priority?
options: Take calcium and antacids 30 minutes before ciprofloxacin to coat the stomach
Ciprofloxacin chelates with calcium and antacids, significantly reducing absorption — take them at least 2 hours apart
These combinations enhance ciprofloxacin absorption — take them together
Antacids are needed to prevent GI irritation from ciprofloxacin — take simultaneously
postTest.rationale: Fluoroquinolones (including ciprofloxacin) form insoluble chelates with divalent and trivalent cations: calcium, magnesium, aluminum (in antacids), iron, and zinc. This dramatically reduces antibiotic absorption and blood levels, potentially causing treatment failure. Separate administration by at least 2 hours (take ciprofloxacin 2 hours before or 6 hours after these agents).

postTest.question: A client is prescribed metronidazole for a C. difficile infection. Which education is highest priority?
options: Take the medication only when you have symptoms — skip doses on symptom-free days
Completely avoid alcohol during treatment and for at least 48 hours after the last dose
You can drink beer but avoid wine — beer has less effect on metronidazole
Take metronidazole on an empty stomach to improve absorption
postTest.rationale: Metronidazole inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase — the enzyme that metabolizes acetaldehyde (a toxic alcohol metabolite). Alcohol combined with metronidazole causes acetaldehyde accumulation → disulfiram-like reaction: severe nausea/vomiting, flushing, headache, palpitations. Even small amounts trigger the reaction. All forms of alcohol must be avoided, including alcohol in mouthwash and cough syrup. The restriction extends 48 hours after the last dose.

lessons.title: Pain management (NCLEX-RN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: Pain management nursing: opioid safety, WHO ladder, addiction vs dependence & NCLEX-RN Canada
lessons.seoDescription: Complete RN lesson on pain management: nociceptive vs neuropathic pain, WHO analgesic ladder, opioid safety monitoring, naloxone, non-pharmacological strategies, and NCLEX-RN priorities.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
**Pain** is defined by the International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP) as 'an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with, or resembling that associated with, actual or potential tissue damage.' Pain is the most common reason patients seek healthcare, and the fifth vital sign — the US Pain Society and CNA recognize that effective pain assessment and management is a core nursing competency.

**Types of pain:**
- **Nociceptive pain**: caused by actual tissue damage. Somatic nociceptive (skin, muscle, bone): well-localized, aching, sharp — fractures, surgical wounds, arthritis. Visceral nociceptive (internal organs): poorly localized, cramping, referred — appendicitis, bowel obstruction, MI.
- **Neuropathic pain**: caused by nerve damage or dysfunction. Burning, shooting, electric, tingling quality. Examples: diabetic neuropathy, post-herpetic neuralgia, spinal cord injury pain, phantom limb pain.
- **Acute pain**: < 3 months; protective function; associated with sympathetic activation (tachycardia, diaphoresis, HTN).
- **Chronic pain**: > 3 months; often no longer protective; frequently involves central sensitization; sympathetic adaptation occurs (may NOT have tachycardia or diaphoresis despite severe pain).

For NCLEX-RN: pain management focuses on: accurate assessment, pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions, opioid safety, and recognizing barriers to pain relief.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology of Pain
sections.body:
**Pain transduction.** Noxious stimuli (mechanical, chemical, thermal) activate peripheral nociceptors — free nerve endings of A-delta fibres (sharp, fast, first pain) and C fibres (dull, aching, slow, second pain). Tissue injury releases algogenic chemicals: prostaglandins (PGE₂), bradykinin, substance P, histamine, ATP. These sensitize nociceptors → peripheral sensitization → lower activation threshold. This is why injured tissue is hypersensitive (allodynia and hyperalgesia).

**Pain transmission.** A-delta and C fibre signals travel to the dorsal horn of the spinal cord and synapse in the substantia gelatinosa (Rexed laminae I, II). Second-order neurons cross the midline and ascend via the spinothalamic tract to the thalamus. The thalamus relays signals to the somatosensory cortex (location and intensity) and limbic system (emotional component — suffering, fear, depression).

**Modulation and gate control.** The gate control theory (Melzack & Wall): A-beta fibres (touch, pressure) can activate inhibitory interneurons in the dorsal horn that 'close the gate' to C-fibre pain signals — this explains why rubbing an injury provides temporary relief, and why TENS and massage work. Descending inhibitory pathways (from periaqueductal gray, raphe nuclei) release endogenous opioids (endorphins, enkephalins), serotonin, and norepinephrine → inhibit pain transmission in the dorsal horn. Opioid drugs mimic endorphins at mu, kappa, and delta receptors.

**Central sensitization.** Repeated or intense pain input 'winds up' the spinal cord — NMDA receptors become sensitized, expanding the pain field and lowering the threshold. This is the mechanism of chronic pain conditions (fibromyalgia, chronic low back pain) and explains why early, aggressive acute pain management helps prevent chronic pain development.

sections.heading: Pain Assessment
sections.body:
**Subjective assessment — self-report is the gold standard:**
- **Numeric Rating Scale (NRS)**: 0–10; 0 = no pain, 10 = worst imaginable
- **Wong-Baker FACES scale**: for children, cognitive impairment, or patients who cannot use numbers
- **PQRSTU mnemonic:**
  - P: Provocation/Palliation — what makes it worse/better?
  - Q: Quality — burning, sharp, dull, cramping, stabbing?
  - R: Region/Radiation — where? Does it spread?
  - S: Severity — 0–10
  - T: Timing — constant, intermittent, onset
  - U: Understanding — what do you think is causing it?

**Objective indicators** (when self-report not possible — behavioural pain scales):
- **CPOT (Critical Care Pain Observation Tool)**: for intubated ICU patients; observes facial expression, body movements, muscle tension, compliance with ventilator or vocalization
- **FLACC scale** (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability): for children < 7 years
- Physiological changes: tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, elevated RR — UNRELIABLE for chronic pain (adaptation occurs); may be present in acute pain

**Barriers to pain assessment:**
- Fear of addiction (patient and provider)
- Cultural stoicism — patient may not report pain
- Communication barriers (language, cognitive impairment)
- Nurses underestimating pain, especially in demographic groups (elderly, non-white patients, patients with substance use history)
- The nurse must BELIEVE the patient's self-report — pain is always subjective

sections.heading: Red Flags / Opioid Safety
sections.body:
**Opioid-induced respiratory depression — notify provider immediately:**
- RR < 8 breaths/min
- SpO₂ < 90% with opioid administration
- Sedation scale > 2 (patient difficult to arouse)
- Pinpoint pupils (miosis) — hallmark of opioid toxicity
- 'Death rattle' or agonal breathing

**OPIOID-RELATED SEDATION SCALE (Pasero):**
- S = Sleeping, easily aroused
- 1 = Awake and alert
- 2 = Slightly drowsy, easily aroused — acceptable, MONITOR
- 3 = Frequently drowsy, arousable, drifts off during conversation — REDUCE DOSE, increase monitoring
- 4 = Somnolent, minimal response to stimulation — STOP OPIOID, consider naloxone

**Naloxone (Narcan) — reversal agent:**
- Indication: opioid-induced respiratory depression (RR < 8, sedation level 3–4, SpO₂ dropping)
- Dose: 0.4 mg diluted in 10 mL NS; give 1–2 mL (0.04–0.08 mg) IV q2–3 min until RR > 8 and patient arousable. Titrate slowly — goal is reversal of respiratory depression, NOT complete reversal of analgesia (which causes acute withdrawal pain and is dangerous in opioid-dependent patients)
- Duration: 30–90 minutes; shorter than most opioids → patient may re-narcotize after naloxone wears off → monitor closely and may need repeat doses or infusion
- Have naloxone and resuscitation equipment available whenever opioids are administered

**NEVER CONFUSE:**
- Addiction (psychological compulsion to use drug despite harm) ≠ Physical dependence (normal physiological adaptation — withdrawal on abrupt cessation) ≠ Tolerance (need for higher dose to achieve same effect). Patients on long-term opioids will have dependence and tolerance — this is NOT addiction.

sections.heading: Diagnostics / Assessment Tools
sections.body:
**Pain assessment tools by population:**
- Adults (alert): NRS 0–10 or VAS
- Children 3–7: FACES pain scale
- Children < 3 or non-verbal: FLACC
- ICU/intubated: CPOT or BPS (Behavioral Pain Scale)
- Neonatal: NIPS (Neonatal Infant Pain Scale), PIPP
- Cognitively impaired elderly: PAINAD (Pain Assessment in Advanced Dementia)

**Documentation:**
- Reassess pain after every intervention: 30–60 minutes after oral medications; 15–30 minutes after IV medications; 1 hour after non-pharmacological intervention
- Document: pain score before AND after treatment, intervention given, patient's functional response

**WHO Analgesic Ladder — 3 steps:**
- Step 1 (mild pain, NRS 1–3): Non-opioids — acetaminophen, NSAIDs (ibuprofen, ketorolac, naproxen)
- Step 2 (moderate pain, NRS 4–6): Weak opioids — codeine, tramadol; ± non-opioid adjuvant
- Step 3 (severe pain, NRS 7–10): Strong opioids — morphine, hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl; ± non-opioid adjuvant

**Labs for pain management monitoring:**
- Renal function: NSAIDs are renally toxic; contraindicated in CKD; dosing adjustment for opioids in renal failure
- Liver function: acetaminophen hepatotoxicity risk in liver disease or > 4g/day; reduce to 2g/day in hepatic impairment
- Serum levels rarely needed unless toxicity suspected (acetaminophen level, opioid toxicology)

sections.heading: Nursing Responsibilities
sections.body:
**Non-pharmacological interventions (always use in conjunction):**
- Positioning: elevate injured extremity, pillow splinting for surgical sites, side-lying for back pain
- Heat: vasodilation, muscle relaxation; use for chronic muscle pain, back pain (NOT on acute inflammation, neuropathy, or areas with impaired sensation)
- Cold (cryotherapy): vasoconstriction, reduces inflammation and swelling; use for acute injuries (first 24–48h), post-procedural swelling
- TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation): gate control mechanism; useful for chronic musculoskeletal pain
- Massage/therapeutic touch
- Distraction, guided imagery, music therapy, mindfulness
- Relaxation breathing
- Acupuncture, acupressure

**Pharmacological interventions — nursing administration:**

**NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, ketorolac):**
- Mechanism: COX inhibition → reduced prostaglandin synthesis → decreased inflammation and pain sensitization
- Monitor: GI bleeding (give with food, PPI for high-risk patients), renal function (hold for eGFR < 30), BP increase, fluid retention
- Ketorolac (Toradol) IV/IM: max 5 days; used post-operatively; avoid in renal failure, GI bleeding, elderly > 65 (increased toxicity)

**Acetaminophen:**
- Safe first-line; max 4g/day in adults with normal liver function; 2g/day in hepatic impairment or heavy alcohol use
- Does NOT have anti-inflammatory effect (unlike NSAIDs) but is an effective analgesic
- Watch for inadvertent overdose (multiple combination products contain acetaminophen)

**Opioid administration:**
- Assess: RR, level of sedation, SpO₂, bowel sounds, pain score BEFORE each dose
- Hold: RR < 10, sedation level ≥ 3 on Pasero scale
- IV opioids: onset 5 min; peak 15–20 min; oral onset 30–60 min, peak 1–2h — time reassessments accordingly
- PCA (Patient Controlled Analgesia): allows patient to self-dose within prescribed limits; only the PATIENT presses the button — never a nurse, family member, or visitor
- Equianalgesic dosing: know conversions for opioid rotation (morphine 10 mg IV = 30 mg PO; hydromorphone 1.5 mg IV = 7.5 mg PO)

**Adjuvant analgesics:**
- Gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin): neuropathic pain; also used for opioid-sparing post-op
- Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline): neuropathic pain; monitor for sedation, anticholinergic effects
- SNRIs (duloxetine): fibromyalgia, diabetic neuropathy
- Topical agents: lidocaine patches, capsaicin cream — for localized neuropathic pain

**Constipation prevention:**
- Start bowel regimen at opioid initiation — do NOT wait for constipation to develop
- Stool softener + stimulant laxative: docusate + senna (Senokot-S)
- Do NOT use fibre/bulk laxatives alone (ineffective without peristalsis from opioids)
- Methylnaltrexone (Relistor): peripheral opioid antagonist; for opioid-induced constipation refractory to laxatives; does NOT cross BBB, does not reverse analgesia

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.body:
- **Believe the patient.** The nurse cannot objectively see or measure pain — the patient's self-report is always the gold standard. Dismissing pain based on absence of objective signs (especially in chronic pain) is both clinically wrong and ethically unacceptable.
- **PCA = patient-controlled only.** Family members pressing the button bypass the safety mechanism. Educate firmly.
- **Naloxone titration**: do NOT give a full 0.4 mg IV bolus — this causes acute withdrawal, reverses all analgesia, and can cause severe pain, vomiting, and tachycardia. Dilute and titrate.
- **Equianalgesic literacy**: when rotating opioids, the equianalgesic dose is a starting point, not an exact conversion — reduce by 25–50% to account for incomplete cross-tolerance.
- **Opioid antagonism half-life**: naloxone 30–90 min; many opioids last 4–6h. After naloxone wears off, re-narcotization can occur — continuous monitoring is essential after administration.
- **NSAIDs and renal risk**: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin production → afferent arteriolar constriction → reduced GFR. In any patient with low volume states (dehydration, HF, sepsis), NSAIDs can cause acute kidney injury rapidly.
- **Multimodal analgesia** (WHO ladder principle): combining drugs with different mechanisms = better pain control with lower doses of each = fewer side effects.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.body:
**Managing pain at home after surgery or acute injury:**
- Take pain medications on a schedule in the first 24–72h — do not wait until pain is severe ('staying ahead of the pain')
- Use non-pharmacological methods alongside medications
- Opioids: do not drive or operate machinery; do not drink alcohol; take stool softeners daily
- Acetaminophen: check all OTC products for acetaminophen content — do not exceed 4g/day total from all sources
- Report: severe nausea/vomiting, difficulty breathing, extreme drowsiness, or complete absence of bowel movement after 3 days

**Safe opioid storage and disposal:**
- Lock opioids away from children and visitors
- Never share opioids — illegal and dangerous
- Dispose unused opioids at pharmacy take-back programs (US drug disposal programs)
- Keep naloxone at home if on chronic opioids: home naloxone kits are available free at most US pharmacies (provincial programs)

**Chronic pain coping strategies:**
- Pain management is not about eliminating pain to zero — it is about improving function
- Pacing activities, goal-setting, and sleep hygiene are foundational
- Psychological support: CBT for chronic pain, acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)
- Physical activity (graded) is evidence-based for almost all chronic pain conditions — immobility worsens chronic pain

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.body:
- Pain is always subjective — believe the patient. Self-report is the gold standard.
- Assess with PQRSTU; reassess within 15–30 min IV or 30–60 min oral after any intervention
- WHO ladder: Step 1 (mild) → non-opioids; Step 2 (moderate) → weak opioids; Step 3 (severe) → strong opioids + adjuvants
- Opioid monitoring: Pasero sedation scale + RR + SpO₂ before each dose. Hold if RR < 10 or sedation ≥ 3.
- Naloxone: dilute 0.4 mg in 10 mL NS; titrate 0.04 mg q2–3 min; monitor for re-narcotization (duration 30–90 min < opioid)
- PCA: ONLY the patient pushes the button
- Constipation: start bowel regimen at opioid initiation; docusate + senna
- Addiction ≠ Dependence ≠ Tolerance — never withhold analgesia due to fear of addiction in patients with legitimate pain

preTest.question: A client post-abdominal surgery rates pain at 8/10 and has RR 14/min, SpO₂ 96%, and is drowsy but arousable to voice. He received IV hydromorphone 30 minutes ago. What is the nurse's priority action?
options: Administer naloxone immediately — the patient has opioid toxicity
Hold the next dose and monitor closely — this is sedation level 2 on the Pasero scale (slightly drowsy, easily aroused); continue monitoring
Increase the dose since pain is still 8/10
Give another full dose in 30 minutes as prescribed — the patient is still in pain
preTest.rationale: Drowsy but easily arousable = Pasero sedation level 2 — acceptable; requires monitoring but not dose withholding or naloxone. RR 14 and SpO₂ 96% are within acceptable limits. Naloxone is for level 3–4 sedation with RR < 8 or SpO₂ < 90%. The nurse should document, continue to monitor, and reassess at the next scheduled dose — pain management decisions beyond monitoring require provider discussion.

preTest.question: A client is prescribed opioids for cancer pain. She tells the nurse, 'I don't want to take these — I'm afraid I'll become addicted.' What is the nurse's best response?
options: Your concern is valid — addiction is a significant risk with opioid use for pain.

Physical dependence (needing the drug to avoid withdrawal) is expected with chronic opioids, but addiction (psychological compulsion despite harm) is very uncommon when opioids are used appropriately for pain management.
You won't need these for long — your cancer pain should go away soon.
If you're worried, we can just use acetaminophen instead.
preTest.rationale: Differentiating addiction from dependence is a key nursing education point. Physical dependence (the body adapts to opioids — withdrawal occurs if stopped abruptly) is expected and does not indicate addiction. Psychological addiction (craving, compulsive use despite harm) is uncommon in patients using opioids for legitimate cancer pain. Validating the fear without providing accurate information delays necessary pain relief and is not therapeutic.

postTest.question: A nurse is administering IV morphine for acute pain. Thirty minutes later, the patient has RR 7/min, SpO₂ 87%, and responds only to vigorous sternal rub. What is the immediate nursing action?
options: Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV push immediately in full dose
Stop the morphine, call a rapid response, administer naloxone diluted in NS titrating 0.04 mg IV q2-3 min until RR > 8
Administer supplemental O₂ and recheck in 10 minutes
Position the patient in left lateral decubitus and elevate legs
postTest.rationale: This is Pasero sedation level 4 with respiratory depression (RR 7, SpO₂ 87%) — opioid toxicity. Actions: STOP the opioid, activate rapid response, administer oxygen, and give DILUTED naloxone titrated to effect. A full 0.4 mg bolus reverses all analgesia abruptly, precipitating acute withdrawal and severe pain in opioid-tolerant patients. Titrating 0.04 mg q2–3 min reverses respiratory depression while preserving some analgesia.

postTest.question: A nurse enters a patient's room and finds the patient's spouse pressing the PCA button 'to help with the pain while he's sleeping.' What is the correct intervention?
options: Allow it — the spouse knows the patient best
Allow one press — the patient is clearly in pain
Immediately educate the spouse that only the patient is to press the PCA button — unauthorized pressing bypasses the safety mechanism and risks respiratory depression
Silence the PCA alarm and increase the basal rate instead
postTest.rationale: PCA safety relies on the patient being awake and conscious enough to press the button — the sedation that precedes respiratory depression prevents a conscious patient from self-dosing. A family member pressing the button on a sleeping patient bypasses this safety mechanism and can cause fatal respiratory depression. The nurse must educate the family immediately and document the intervention.

postTest.question: Which statement best describes the correct acetaminophen dosing principle for a client with chronic alcohol use?
options: Acetaminophen is contraindicated in all patients who drink alcohol — use NSAIDs instead
Maximum dose is reduced to 2g/day due to increased risk of hepatotoxicity in patients with liver impairment from alcohol use
Acetaminophen should be given at the full 4g/day dose to avoid using opioids
The patient's pain is self-induced — acetaminophen is not indicated
postTest.rationale: Acetaminophen is hepatotoxic at high doses and the toxic threshold is lower in patients with liver impairment (including chronic alcohol use). The maximum dose should be reduced to 2g/day. Acetaminophen is not absolutely contraindicated in alcohol use — it remains safer than NSAIDs (which worsen GI bleeding risk and renal function in this population).

lessons.title: Wound care (NCLEX-RN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: Wound care nursing: pressure injuries, wound assessment, dressings & NCLEX-RN Canada
lessons.seoDescription: Complete RN lesson on wound care: pressure injury staging, wound assessment, dressing types, infection signs, healing phases, and NCLEX-RN clinical priorities.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
**Wound care** spans everything from surgical incisions and traumatic lacerations to pressure injuries, diabetic foot ulcers, and venous or arterial ulcers. For NCLEX-RN, the highest-yield areas are: (1) pressure injury staging and prevention, (2) wound assessment (size, depth, exudate, wound bed, periwound skin), (3) selection of appropriate dressing types, and (4) recognition of wound infection.

Wound healing follows a predictable sequence (hemostasis → inflammation → proliferation → remodeling) and can be disrupted by infection, poor perfusion, malnutrition, diabetes, and immunosuppression. The nurse's role is to create and maintain an environment that supports optimal healing, prevent complications, and educate patients on wound self-management.

sections.heading: Pressure Injury Staging and Pathophysiology
sections.body:
**Pressure injury pathophysiology.** Pressure injuries (formerly called pressure ulcers or decubitus ulcers) result from prolonged pressure that compresses microvascular capillaries, reducing perfusion to the skin and underlying tissue. Ischemia begins within hours on bony prominences (sacrum, heels, occiput, greater trochanter, malleoli, ischial tuberosities). Contributing factors: shear forces (skin moving over underlying tissue during repositioning), friction, moisture (incontinence-associated dermatitis destroys the protective skin barrier), malnutrition, and immobility.

**NPUAP/EPUAP Pressure Injury Staging:**

- **Stage 1**: Non-blanchable erythema of intact skin. Skin is intact but does not turn white when pressed — indicates early tissue damage. Most visible on lighter skin; darker skin may show discoloration, warmth, or firmness.

- **Stage 2**: Partial thickness skin loss. Loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with pink/red wound bed OR intact/open/ruptured serum-filled blister. No slough or eschar. Do NOT call blisters from tape, incontinence, or moisture 'Stage 2' — these are not pressure injuries.

- **Stage 3**: Full thickness skin loss. Subcutaneous fat visible. Slough or eschar may be present. May have tunneling or undermining. Bone, tendon, and muscle are NOT visible — if they were, it would be Stage 4.

- **Stage 4**: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough and/or eschar may be present. Deep undermining and tunneling common. High risk for osteomyelitis.

- **Unstageable**: Full thickness skin/tissue loss where the extent cannot be determined because the wound is covered by slough or eschar that cannot be removed without debridement.

- **Deep Tissue Pressure Injury (DTPI)**: Intact or non-intact skin with persistent non-blanchable deep red, maroon, or purple discoloration. May indicate soft tissue injury from pressure/shear occurring below the surface — may evolve rapidly to Stage 3 or 4.

sections.heading: Wound Assessment & Infection Recognition
sections.body:
**Systematic wound assessment (document every dressing change):**
- Location (anatomical description)
- Size: length × width × depth in centimeters
- Wound bed: granulation (red/pink — healthy), slough (yellow/tan/grey — necrotic fibrin), eschar (black/brown — dead tissue), epithelial tissue (pink/white — healing)
- Exudate: type (serous, serosanguineous, purulent), amount (minimal, moderate, large), odor
- Wound edges: attached vs undermining/tunneling; assess direction (clock face notation)
- Periwound skin: erythema (extending from wound edge), maceration (white, soft — moisture damage), induration, warmth
- Pain: location, character, severity — new or worsening pain in a chronic wound = infection or ischemia signal

**Signs of wound infection (NERDS for superficial; STONES for deep):**
- NERDS (superficial critical colonization): Non-healing, Exudate increase, Red/bleeding granulation, Debris, Smell
- STONES (deep infection): Size increasing, Temperature elevated, Os (bone probe positive/osteomyelitis), New breakdown, Erythema/edema, Smell, Exudate

**General wound infection signs:**
- Purulent drainage (green/yellow/thick)
- Surrounding erythema > 2 cm from wound edge and extending
- Warmth and induration
- Fever, leukocytosis (systemic signs)
- Increasing pain in a previously comfortable wound

sections.heading: Dressing Selection Guide
sections.body:
**Wound dressing selection is based on wound moisture needs:**

**Dry wounds (need moisture):**
- **Hydrogel**: adds moisture to dry, necrotic wounds; promotes autolytic debridement
- **Transparent film**: maintains moisture; useful for Stage 1 pressure injuries and superficial wounds without exudate; allows visualization without removal

**Moist wounds with moderate exudate:**
- **Hydrocolloid**: absorbs moderate exudate; maintains moist environment; do not use over infected wounds or deep wounds; common for Stage 2 pressure injuries
- **Foam dressings**: highly absorbent; good for moderate-heavy exudate wounds; provide cushioning over bony prominences

**High exudate wounds:**
- **Alginate dressings**: made from seaweed; highly absorbent; manage heavy exudate (e.g. deep ulcers, heavily draining wounds); require secondary dressing

**Infected wounds:**
- **Silver-containing dressings** (silver foam, silver alginate): antimicrobial; used for infected or critically colonized wounds; not for clean, healing wounds (toxic to new granulation tissue)
- **Iodosorb/Iodine gauze**: antimicrobial for infected wounds

**General rules:**
- Do NOT use occlusive dressings (hydrocolloid, film) over infected wounds or tunnels
- Change dressings per order and when soiled, saturated, or disturbed
- Use sterile technique for new surgical wounds and clean technique for chronic wounds per agency policy

sections.heading: Nursing Responsibilities
sections.body:
**Pressure injury prevention (priority: every admission):**
- Complete validated skin risk assessment on admission (Braden Scale or Waterlow score) and re-assess per policy
- Reposition every 2 hours (bed) or every 1 hour (chair) — clock document
- Protect bony prominences: foam wedges, heel offloading boots, pressure-redistribution mattress
- Keep skin clean and dry: incontinence-associated dermatitis → protective barrier cream immediately
- Nutrition consult for patients at risk: protein intake critical for wound healing

**Wound care procedure:**
- Verify wound care order (type of dressing, solution, frequency)
- Gather all supplies before beginning
- Apply appropriate PPE (gloves; sterile gloves for new surgical wounds)
- Clean wound per order: most wounds — normal saline irrigation; infected wounds — per protocol
- Measure wound at each assessment; photograph if policy allows
- Apply ordered dressing; secure without restricting circulation
- Document assessment and procedure

**Pain management during wound care:**
- Pre-medicate 30–45 minutes before dressing change if prescribed
- Use time-outs if patient requests during procedure
- Non-pharmacologic: distraction, positioning for comfort, warming irrigation solution

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.body:
- **Heels are the second most common pressure injury site** (after sacrum). Offload heels in ALL bedridden patients — this is a nursing standard, not an order-dependent action.
- **Stage cannot be reverse-staged** — a Stage 3 wound healing does not 'become' Stage 1 as it heals. Document as 'Stage 3, healing' and describe the wound bed accurately.
- **Unstageable ≠ Stage 4** — Unstageable means the staging cannot be determined without removing the eschar/slough. Do NOT call it Stage 4 by default.
- **Moist wound healing** is the gold standard: wounds heal faster in a moist environment than in a dry one. Do not allow wounds to dry out between dressing changes.
- **Protein is the key nutrition factor** for wound healing: minimum 1.25–1.5 g/kg/day in wound healing patients. Assess albumin/prealbumin as indicators.
- **Wound cultures**: swab cultures of wound exudate identify surface colonizers, not deep tissue bacteria. Use a quantitative biopsy or deep-tissue swab technique for reliable results — consult wound care specialist.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.body:
**For patients being discharged with wounds or at risk for pressure injuries:**
- Inspect skin daily: use a mirror for areas you can't see directly (heels, sacrum)
- If you see a red area that does not go away after 30 minutes of offloading the pressure, report immediately
- Reposition frequently: if you're in a wheelchair or chair, shift weight every 30 minutes
- Keep skin dry: manage incontinence promptly; use barrier cream on at-risk skin

**Wound self-care at home:**
- Follow dressing change instructions exactly — when to change, how to clean, type of dressing
- Infection warning signs to report: increasing pain, odor, warmth, spreading redness, pus
- Keep follow-up appointments for wound assessment
- Nutrition: adequate protein (lean meats, eggs, legumes, dairy) supports healing
- Diabetic patients: blood glucose control is essential for wound healing — hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil function and tissue repair

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.body:
- Pressure injuries: caused by sustained pressure + shear → ischemia. Stage 1–4, Unstageable, Deep Tissue PI. Never reverse-stage.
- Prevention: reposition q2h (bed), q1h (chair), offload heels, skin barrier products, risk assessment (Braden Scale), nutrition
- Wound assessment: LOCATION, SIZE, BED (granulation/slough/eschar), EXUDATE, EDGES, PERIWOUND SKIN, PAIN
- Infection signs: purulent drainage, surrounding erythema > 2 cm, warmth, fever, leukocytosis, new/worsening pain
- Dressing selection: dry wound → hydrogel; moderate exudate → hydrocolloid/foam; heavy exudate → alginate; infected → silver-containing
- Moist wound healing > dry: never let wounds desiccate between dressing changes

preTest.question: A 60-year-old female patient with a history of diabetes is admitted with a foot ulcer that shows signs of infection. What is the most appropriate initial nursing intervention?
options: Clean the ulcer and apply a sterile dressing.
Notify the healthcare provider about the infection.
Assess the patient's blood glucose levels.
Teach the patient about foot care and hygiene.
preTest.rationale: Notifying the healthcare provider about the infection is the most appropriate initial intervention, as the patient may require systemic antibiotics and further evaluation to prevent complications. While cleaning the ulcer and assessing blood glucose levels are important, they do not address the immediate need for medical intervention.

preTest.question: A 58-year-old female patient with a history of diabetes presents with a foot ulcer that has not healed for several weeks. What is the most appropriate nursing intervention?
options: Apply a moist dressing to the ulcer.
Schedule a consult with a wound care specialist.
Teach the patient about foot care and hygiene.
Assess the patient's blood glucose levels.
preTest.rationale: For a non-healing foot ulcer, consulting with a wound care specialist is essential to assess the wound and provide specialized treatment options. While applying a moist dressing is important for wound care, it is not as critical as getting specialized input for a chronic issue. Teaching about foot care is necessary but not the priority when the ulcer is not healing. Assessing blood glucose levels is also relevant but does not directly address the non-healing ulcer.

preTest.question: A client with a stage 3 pressure injury has a wound bed with 80% red granulation tissue and 20% yellow slough. The nurse should:
options: Apply a dry gauze dressing
Use autolytic debridement with a moisture-retentive dressing
Pack the wound tightly with gauze
Apply povidone-iodine to all wound tissue
preTest.rationale: Wounds with mixed tissue types benefit from autolytic debridement using moisture-retentive dressings (hydrogel, hydrocolloid) that soften slough while protecting granulation tissue. Mechanical debridement may damage healthy tissue. Povidone-iodine is cytotoxic to new tissue.

preTest.question: A nurse is delegating tasks for the shift. Which task is appropriate to delegate to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
options: Teaching a newly diagnosed diabetic client how to check blood glucose
Obtaining vital signs on a stable postoperative client
Assessing a client's surgical wound for signs of infection
Administering a scheduled oral pain medication
preTest.rationale: Obtaining vital signs on a stable client is a routine task within the UAP scope of practice. Teaching requires assessment and clinical judgment and must be performed by the RN. Wound assessment requires nursing judgment to evaluate for complications. Medication administration cannot be delegated to UAP (in most jurisdictions). The RN must ensure the task is appropriate, the client is stable, and proper supervision is available.

preTest.question: A client has a wound VAC (vacuum-assisted closure) device. The nurse should ensure:
options: The dressing is removed daily for wound assessment
Continuous negative pressure is maintained and the seal is intact
The tubing is clamped when the client ambulates
The device is turned off at night
preTest.rationale: Wound VAC maintains continuous or intermittent negative pressure to promote wound healing. Ensure: airtight seal (no air leaks), continuous suction (alarm sounds if seal breaks), tubing is not kinked, canister is not full, and assess wound per facility protocol (typically every 48-72 hours at dressing change). Do not clamp tubing.

postTest.question: A nurse is delegating care for four clients to an unregulated care provider (UCP). Which task is outside the UCP's scope and must be retained by the nurse?
options: Assessing a client's wound for signs of infection and determining if the treatment plan needs modification
Obtaining vital signs on a stable client as directed by the nurse
Assisting a client with bathing and personal hygiene
Transporting a stable client to the radiology department by wheelchair
postTest.rationale: Wound assessment and clinical judgment about treatment plan modifications require professional nursing knowledge, critical thinking, and clinical decision-making that cannot be delegated to unregulated care providers. The five rights of delegation are: right task, right circumstance, right person, right direction/communication, and right supervision. UCPs can perform tasks such as vital signs collection, hygiene assistance, and transport for stable clients. The nurse retains responsibility for assessment, evaluation, and clinical judgment.

postTest.question: A client presenting with flank pain, hematuria, and a palpable abdominal mass should be evaluated for:
options: Simple renal cyst
Renal cell carcinoma (classic triad, though present in only 10% of patients)
Urinary tract infection
Benign prostatic hyperplasia
postTest.rationale: RCC classic triad (hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass) occurs in only 10% of patients. Many are found incidentally on imaging. Other features: weight loss, paraneoplastic syndromes (polycythemia from erythropoietin, hypercalcemia from PTHrP). CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the staging study.

postTest.question: A nurse is caring for an elderly patient with a stage III pressure ulcer. The patient has a history of diabetes. Which intervention is the priority to promote healing and prevent infection?
options: Change the dressing according to the schedule.
Educate the patient on diabetes management.
Encourage adequate protein intake.
Reposition the patient every two hours.
postTest.rationale: Repositioning the patient every two hours is the priority intervention to relieve pressure on the ulcer site, which is essential to promote healing and prevent further tissue damage and infection. While changing the dressing, educating about diabetes management, and encouraging protein intake are also critical, they are secondary to addressing the mechanical factor causing the ulcer.

postTest.question: A client with borderline personality disorder cuts themselves during hospitalization. The nurse's best response is:
options: Tell the client they are seeking attention
Provide wound care and calmly process the event with the client
Apply four-point restraints
Discharge the client for contract violation
postTest.rationale: Self-harm in BPD is a maladaptive coping mechanism for intense emotions. The nurse provides non-judgmental wound care, assesses for suicidal intent, explores triggers, and works with the client on alternative coping strategies.

postTest.question: A client has a wound with yellow, stringy tissue covering the wound bed. The practical nurse documents this tissue as which of the following?
options: Slough
Eschar
Granulation tissue
Epithelial tissue
postTest.rationale: Slough is yellow, tan, or gray stringy tissue composed of dead cells and debris that covers the wound bed. It must often be removed to promote healing. Eschar is dry, thick, black or brown necrotic tissue. Granulation tissue is red, moist, and beefy, indicating healthy healing. Epithelial tissue is new pink skin growing from the wound edges.

postTest.question: Which multidisciplinary team member should be consulted for a complex Wound Infection case?
options: Administrative staff
Specialist appropriate for Immunology expertise in managing Wound Infection
Chaplain as first consult
Physical therapy only
postTest.rationale: Complex Wound Infection cases benefit from specialist expertise within the Immunology domain for optimal management.

postTest.question: A client with melanoma has a Breslow depth of 2.5mm. The nurse should understand this indicates:
options: Low risk of metastasis
Intermediate risk requiring wide excision with 2cm margins and sentinel lymph node biopsy
A superficial, easily treated lesion
No need for further evaluation
postTest.rationale: Breslow depth of 2.5mm (intermediate thickness, 1-4mm) requires wide excision with 2cm margins and sentinel lymph node biopsy for staging. Five-year survival is approximately 80%. Regular follow-up with dermatological examinations is essential.

postTest.question: A patient with a recent history of a surgical wound is being assessed. The nurse notes that the wound is red, warm to touch, and there is purulent drainage. What is the most likely complication of the wound?
options: Dehiscence
Infection
Hematoma
Necrosis
postTest.rationale: The signs of redness, warmth, and purulent drainage indicate a probable infection (B). Dehiscence (A) is a separation of the wound edges, which is not indicated here. A hematoma (C) would typically present as a localized collection of blood outside of blood vessels, often bluish in color, and necrosis (D) refers to tissue death, which does not correlate with the symptoms presented. Thus, B is the correct choice.

postTest.question: A nurse is providing first aid to a client who has been bitten by a snake. Which action is appropriate?
options: Remove jewelry near the bite, immobilize the affected extremity at or below heart level, and seek immediate medical care
Apply a tourniquet above the bite to prevent venom spread
Cut the wound and attempt to suck out the venom
Apply ice directly to the bite wound
postTest.rationale: Appropriate snakebite management includes removing constricting items (jewelry, watches) before swelling occurs, immobilizing the extremity at or below heart level, and transporting for antivenom therapy. Tourniquets can cause tissue necrosis. Cutting and suctioning is ineffective and increases tissue damage. Ice causes vasoconstriction and local tissue injury without affecting venom distribution.

postTest.question: A client presents with a wound that has a foul odor and green drainage. This is consistent with:
options: Normal wound healing
Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection
Staphylococcal infection
Candida infection
postTest.rationale: Pseudomonas aeruginosa produces green-pigmented (pyocyanin) drainage with a characteristically fruity or foul odor. It is commonly found in burn wounds, chronic wounds, and moist environments. Treatment includes anti-pseudomonal antibiotics.

lessons.title: Delegation & assignment (NCLEX-RN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: Delegation & assignment nursing: RN, RPN, UAP scope & NCLEX-RN Canada
lessons.seoDescription: Complete RN lesson on delegation: five rights of delegation, RN vs RPN vs UAP scope, assessment delegation rules, and NCLEX-RN clinical judgment priorities.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
**Delegation** is the transfer of authority to perform a nursing task to a competent individual while retaining accountability for the outcome. It is one of the most frequently tested topics on NCLEX-RN because errors in delegation cause patient harm and represent failures of professional nursing judgment.

In Canada, delegation occurs within the framework of provincial regulatory bodies (e.g. CNO in Ontario, BCCNPS in BC) and employer policy. The RN retains accountability regardless of who performs the delegated task. The critical rule: **delegation never transfers accountability** — the RN is always responsible for appropriate task assignment and patient outcome.

Delegation must be distinguished from **assignment** (directing a team member to perform tasks within their own scope) — you assign tasks to RNs/RPNs; you delegate tasks outside the normal scope to unlicensed personnel or other regulated staff.

sections.heading: The Five Rights of Delegation
sections.body:
The **Five Rights of Delegation** provide the decision framework the RN must apply before any delegation:

1. **Right Task**: Is this task appropriate to delegate for this patient? Tasks with predictable outcomes, standardized procedures, and minimal need for nursing judgment are delegable. Assessment, care planning, evaluation, and teaching are NOT delegable.

2. **Right Circumstance**: Is this patient stable enough for delegation? Unstable patients (new, changing, or complex conditions) require RN direct care. Delegation is appropriate for stable, chronic patients with predictable care needs.

3. **Right Person**: Does this person have the documented competency and licensure/certification to perform this task? Match the complexity of the task to the level of the provider. Example: vital signs → UAP; IV medication → RN; wound assessment → RN, not UAP.

4. **Right Direction/Communication**: Did the RN give clear, specific instructions including what to do, when to do it, and what to report back? Vague delegation is inadequate delegation.

5. **Right Supervision/Evaluation**: Is the RN monitoring the outcome and available to be notified of changes? Delegation without follow-through is abandonment.

sections.heading: Scope of Practice: What Can Be Delegated and to Whom
sections.body:
**CANNOT be delegated by RN to anyone (RN-only):**
- Initial patient assessment (head-to-toe on admission or for new/unstable patients)
- Nursing diagnosis and care planning
- Care plan evaluation
- Patient and family teaching (initial; basic reinforcement may be delegated in some settings)
- Medication administration via complex routes (IV push, epidural, intrathecal)
- Insertion of NG tubes, central lines, chest tubes
- Care of unstable patients requiring frequent reassessment

**CAN be delegated to RPNs/LPNs (within their scope):**
- Medication administration via established peripheral IV, oral, topical, SQ
- Catheter insertion and care
- Wound care (stable wounds per policy)
- Focused assessments for stable patients with predictable outcomes
- Documentation as per scope

**CAN be delegated to UAP (unlicensed assistive personnel — PSW, HCA, CNA):**
- Vital signs (stable, routine patients)
- Blood glucose monitoring (routine, stable — not for hypoglycemia protocol)
- Ambulation assistance
- ADL assistance: bathing, dressing, feeding (stable patients)
- Intake and output recording
- Weighing patients
- Specimen collection (urine via collection hat — not catheter insertion)

**Cannot delegate to UAP:**
- Assessment or interpretation of findings
- Medication administration of any kind
- Care for unstable, new, or complex patients
- Any skill requiring nursing judgment

sections.heading: Unsafe Delegation — Recognize and Refuse
sections.body:
**The RN must refuse or correct these delegation patterns:**

- **Delegating assessment**: 'Tell me if he looks worse' is NOT appropriate delegation. Assessment is an RN function. The UAP observes; the RN assesses.
- **Delegating unstable patients**: A client with new-onset chest pain, acute deterioration, or a recent procedure cannot have care delegated to UAP or even RPN for complex needs.
- **Delegating without direction**: Handing off a patient without specifying what to do, what to watch for, and what to report is negligent.
- **Delegating to avoid accountability**: If the outcome of a task requires nursing judgment (e.g. 'take vitals and adjust the IV rate if BP drops'), you cannot delegate this as a single task.
- **Accepting delegation that exceeds your scope**: As an RN, you must also not accept tasks outside your own knowledge base or competency — scope has upper limits too.
- **Over-delegating from workload pressure**: Feeling overwhelmed does not make unsafe delegation acceptable. Escalate to charge nurse if assignment load is unsafe.

sections.heading: Nursing Responsibilities
sections.body:
**Before delegating:**
- Apply the Five Rights of Delegation to every task
- Verify the delegate's competency (not just licensure — verify current skills and familiarity with the specific patient)
- Ensure the patient is stable enough for delegated care
- Set a clear time frame for the task and reporting

**Communication standard:**
- Give specific direction: 'Please take vital signs on Mr. Smith in Room 412 every 30 minutes and report immediately if systolic BP drops below 100 or heart rate goes above 110.'
- Avoid vague instruction: 'Keep an eye on the patient in 412.'
- Use SBAR (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) for verbal hand-offs

**After delegating:**
- Follow up on the delegated task before end of shift
- Evaluate the patient directly — do not rely solely on the delegate's report when assessment is indicated
- Document your supervision: the RN signs for oversight, not just the task performer

**In the US context:**
- Provincial college standards (e.g. CNO, CRNBC) define regulated controlled acts and who may perform them
- Employer policies and position descriptions further define assignment scope within regulated boundaries
- When in doubt, consult the provincial regulatory document or charge nurse

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.body:
- **NCLEX rule**: 'Assessment' is always RN-only. If an answer option involves any assessment function, UAPs or RPNs should not be assigned it.
- **Stable = predictable, chronic, uncomplicated.** If any of those conditions change — patient becomes unstable, new complaint arises, recent intervention — return to direct RN care.
- **Delegating is not avoiding work** — the RN must still supervise, evaluate, and remain responsible for outcomes. You cannot 'give away' accountability.
- **Prioritize the RN's direct care time** on: unstable patients, complex assessments, teaching for understanding, complex medication administration, psychosocial care requiring therapeutic communication.
- **Charge nurse escalation**: if the assignment itself is unsafe (too many unstable patients, inadequate staffing), the RN must communicate this professionally and document the concern — do not silently accept unsafe conditions.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.body:
**For patients and families regarding the care team:**
- Explain who will be providing care: RN, RPN, and support staff each have specific roles
- Reassure that the registered nurse is always accountable for care planning and assessment
- Encourage patients to report changes in their condition to any member of the team — an RN will be notified immediately for clinical changes
- Patients have the right to ask which provider is caring for them and their scope of practice
- If a patient expresses concern about a task being performed by a support worker, acknowledge the concern and clarify the delegation was appropriate — or reassess if it was not

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.body:
- Delegation = transfer of task authority; the RN retains accountability always
- Five Rights: right task, right circumstance, right person, right direction, right supervision
- Never delegate: assessment, care planning, evaluation, teaching, unstable patient care, complex medication routes
- UAP can: vital signs, ADLs, I&O, ambulation assistance, blood glucose (routine/stable), specimen collection
- Unsafe delegation: assigning assessment to UAP, delegating unstable patients, delegating without direction or follow-up
- NCLEX key: if the option involves assessment, it stays with the RN regardless of how small it seems

preTest.question: A nurse is supervising a nursing assistant who is preparing to assist a 45-year-old male patient with a history of obesity and sleep apnea in getting out of bed. What should the nurse instruct the assistant to do first?
options: Use a gait belt for safety.
Ensure the patient is awake and alert.
Assess the patient's mobility level.
Encourage the patient to use a walker.
preTest.rationale: Ensuring that the patient is awake and alert is crucial before any physical assistance is provided. This addresses patient safety and readiness for movement. While using a gait belt is important and assessing mobility is necessary, these actions should come after confirming the patient's alertness. Encouraging the use of a walker is also relevant, but again, it is secondary to ensuring the patient is awake and able to cooperate.

preTest.question: A practical nurse is asked by a physician to administer a medication that is unfamiliar. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Look up the medication's indications, dose, route, and side effects before administering it
Administer the medication because the physician ordered it
Refuse to administer the medication without explanation
Ask another nurse to administer the medication instead
preTest.rationale: Nurses have an independent obligation to ensure safe medication administration. Looking up an unfamiliar medication allows the nurse to verify the indication, appropriate dose, route, and potential adverse effects. Administering blindly based on a physician order does not meet the standard of safe practice. Refusing without explanation or delegating does not resolve the knowledge gap.

preTest.question: The RPN delegates vital sign measurement to unlicensed assistive personnel. The RPN remains responsible for:
options: Nothing once delegated
Supervising the task and evaluating the results
Only documenting the delegation
Performing the task themselves
preTest.rationale: Delegation does not transfer accountability. The RPN retains responsibility for: ensuring the task is appropriate for the UAP, providing clear instructions, supervising performance, evaluating results, and intervening if values are abnormal. The five rights of delegation guide this process.

preTest.question: A nurse is caring for a terminally ill client who has expressed a desire to discontinue all treatments, including IV fluids and antibiotics. The client's family is insisting that all treatments be continued. What is the nurse's primary obligation?
options: Follow the family's wishes since they have the client's best interests in mind
Discharge the client against medical advice to resolve the conflict
Continue all treatments until an ethics committee makes a decision
Advocate for the client's autonomous decision by communicating the client's wishes to the healthcare team and facilitating a family conference
preTest.rationale: The ethical principle of autonomy gives a competent adult client the right to refuse any and all treatments, including life-sustaining measures, regardless of the family's wishes. The nurse's primary obligation is to advocate for the client's expressed preferences. This includes clearly documenting the client's wishes, communicating them to the healthcare team, and facilitating a family conference where the provider, social worker, chaplain, and palliative care team can help the family understand and accept the client's decision. The nurse should also ensure the client has been fully informed of the consequences of treatment withdrawal (informed consent/refusal). Simply following the family's preferences violates the client's autonomy. Waiting for an ethics committee, while sometimes appropriate, should not delay honoring the client's current decision. Discharge against medical advice is inappropriate and does not resolve the underlying ethical conflict.

preTest.question: A nurse is working on a unit that is short-staffed. The charge nurse asks the RN to float to the labor and delivery unit for the shift. The nurse has no experience in obstetric nursing. What is the appropriate response?
options: Refuse to float because the assignment is unsafe and leave the unit
Accept the assignment and rely on the labor and delivery nurses for guidance throughout the shift
Communicate concerns to the charge nurse, request an orientation or assignment to tasks within competency, and document the communication
File a formal complaint with the state board of nursing before accepting the assignment
preTest.rationale: When asked to float to an unfamiliar unit, the nurse should communicate competency concerns to the charge nurse and request an assignment appropriate to their skill level. The nurse can perform tasks within their general competency (vital signs, medication administration, basic assessments) while specialized tasks (fetal monitoring interpretation, labor management) should remain with experienced L&D nurses. Documentation of the communication protects both the nurse and the patients. Refusing and leaving constitutes patient abandonment once the nurse has accepted the assignment. Blindly accepting without communicating limitations puts patients at risk and exposes the nurse to liability for practicing outside their competency. Filing a complaint before attempting resolution through appropriate channels is premature.

postTest.question: A practical nurse working in a long-term care facility is asked to perform a procedure they have not been trained to do. What is the correct action?
options: Decline to perform the procedure and notify the supervisor that additional training is required
Attempt the procedure using online video instructions
Perform the procedure because the assignment was given by the supervisor
Ask an unregulated care provider to perform the procedure instead
postTest.rationale: Nurses must only perform procedures within their competence. Declining and notifying the supervisor allows for alternative arrangements and identifies a training need. Attempting an unfamiliar procedure using videos risks client harm. Supervisor assignment does not override competency requirements. Delegating a procedure the nurse cannot perform to an unregulated provider is inappropriate and unsafe.

postTest.question: A practical nurse is caring for a confused client who repeatedly attempts to climb out of bed. The nurse has exhausted all least-restrictive alternatives. Which action should the nurse take before applying a restraint?
options: Obtain a healthcare provider order for the specific type of restraint
Apply the restraint and document the rationale afterward
Ask a family member for verbal consent
Delegate the restraint application to the personal support worker
postTest.rationale: Restraint application requires a healthcare provider order specifying the type of restraint, indication, and duration. Applying restraints without an order violates client rights and institutional policy. Family consent does not replace a provider order. Restraint application must be performed by a regulated health professional, not delegated to unregulated staff.

postTest.question: An RPN/LPN receives an order that seems inappropriate. The nurse should:
options: Follow the order as written since the provider gave it
Clarify the order with the prescribing provider before carrying it out
Refuse to follow the order without explanation
Ask a colleague to carry out the order instead
postTest.rationale: Nurses are professionally and legally responsible for the care they provide. If an order seems inappropriate, unclear, or potentially harmful: verify with the prescribing provider, provide rationale for concern, document the communication, and follow the chain of command if the concern is not resolved. Never blindly follow an unsafe order.

postTest.question: A practical nurse is asked to float to an unfamiliar unit. What is the nurse's first responsibility upon arrival?
options: Communicate competency limitations to the charge nurse and clarify which tasks are within the nurse's scope
Accept all assignments without question to be cooperative
Refuse to float because the assignment is unfamiliar
Perform all tasks as assigned regardless of training
postTest.rationale: When floating to an unfamiliar unit, the nurse must communicate any scope or competency limitations to the charge nurse so that safe client assignments can be made. Accepting all tasks without clarification risks client harm if the nurse lacks competency. Refusing to float may not be an option per institutional policy. Performing tasks without adequate training is unsafe.

postTest.question: The RPN should NOT delegate which type of task?
options: Bathing and hygiene care
Initial client assessment and nursing diagnosis formulation
Routine vital sign measurement on stable clients
Ambulation of a stable client
postTest.rationale: Initial assessment and nursing diagnosis require professional nursing judgment and are within the registered nurse scope. RPNs can perform assessments within their scope but initial comprehensive assessment and care plan development are RN responsibilities.

postTest.question: A nurse is the charge nurse on a busy medical-surgical unit during a night shift. One RN calls in sick, leaving the unit short-staffed. The remaining nurses each have 8 clients. A new admission is en route from the emergency department. What should the charge nurse do?
options: Refuse the admission because the unit is over capacity
Accept the admission, assign the client to the nurse with the most stable patients, adjust workloads if possible, and notify the nursing supervisor of the staffing concern
Close the unit to all admissions without notifying administration
Assign all admissions to the least experienced nurse to help them develop skills
postTest.rationale: The charge nurse must balance patient safety with operational needs. Accepting the admission while strategically assigning the new client to the nurse whose existing patients are most stable ensures safe care distribution. Notifying the nursing supervisor documents the staffing concern through proper channels and may result in additional resources such as a float nurse or agency staff. Refusing an admission from the ED can jeopardize patient safety by keeping a sick client in an overcrowded emergency department. Closing the unit without administrative approval exceeds the charge nurse's authority. Assigning all admissions to the least experienced nurse creates an unsafe patient care situation and is not appropriate delegation or workload management.

postTest.question: The RPN/LPN should recognize that which task cannot be delegated to unlicensed assistive personnel?
options: Measuring vital signs on a stable client
Administering medications
Assisting with bathing and hygiene
Measuring intake and output
postTest.rationale: Medication administration requires a licensed nurse (RN, RPN/LPN). It involves assessment, clinical judgment, knowledge of pharmacology, and potential for adverse effects. UAP tasks: vital signs on stable clients, I&O measurement, ambulation, bathing, feeding (non-complex), and specimen collection.

postTest.question: A charge nurse receives report that the unit is short-staffed with one RN, one LPN, and one unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) for 12 clients. Which assignment is most appropriate?
options: RN takes the most complex/unstable clients, LPN takes stable medical clients, UAP assists with activities of daily living
UAP takes the most stable clients independently including medication administration
LPN performs all initial assessments while the RN handles administrative tasks
All three staff members share clients equally regardless of complexity
postTest.rationale: Safe delegation follows the principle of matching client needs with provider competency. The RN must care for unstable, complex clients requiring assessment, clinical judgment, and evaluation. The LPN can care for stable clients with predictable outcomes, performing delegated nursing tasks. The UAP can perform tasks such as vital signs, ADL assistance, and ambulation under supervision. UAPs cannot administer medications or perform assessments. Equal distribution ignores scope of practice.

postTest.question: The RPN should NOT delegate which task to unlicensed assistive personnel?
options: Taking vital signs
Initial assessment and clinical judgment-based decision making
Ambulation assistance
Feeding assistance
postTest.rationale: The nurse retains tasks requiring professional nursing judgment: initial and ongoing assessment, care planning, nursing diagnosis, evaluation of outcomes, teaching, and any task requiring clinical decision-making. UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require professional judgment.

postTest.question: When delegating to UAP, the nurse must ensure the task is:
options: Beyond the UAP's training level to promote learning
Within the UAP's scope of practice, the client's condition is stable, and proper supervision is available
Anything the nurse does not want to do
A complex assessment task
postTest.rationale: Five Rights of Delegation: Right task (routine, predictable outcome), Right circumstance (stable client, predictable environment), Right person (competent, trained UAP), Right direction/communication (clear, concise instructions), Right supervision (monitor, evaluate, provide feedback).

lessons.title: Fluid deficit vs excess (NCLEX-RN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: Fluid balance nursing: IV fluids, dehydration, overload, third-spacing & NCLEX-RN Canada
lessons.seoDescription: Complete RN lesson on fluid balance: isotonic vs hypotonic vs hypertonic IV fluids, fluid volume deficit and excess, third-spacing, I&O, assessment, and NCLEX-RN priorities.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
**Fluid balance** is a foundational nursing competency. Maintaining homeostasis requires precise management of fluid intake and output, electrolyte balance, and vascular volume. For NCLEX-RN Canada, fluid balance knowledge is tested in virtually every clinical specialization — from cardiac and renal nursing to surgical care, obstetrics, and critical care.

**Body fluid compartments:**
- Total body water (TBW): ~60% of body weight in adult men, ~50% in women
  - Intracellular fluid (ICF): 2/3 of TBW — inside cells
  - Extracellular fluid (ECF): 1/3 of TBW — outside cells
    - Intravascular: plasma (within blood vessels)
    - Interstitial: between cells and vessels (lymphatics)
    - Transcellular: CSF, pleural, pericardial, peritoneal, synovial fluid

**Osmolality controls fluid movement:**
- Water moves from areas of low osmolality → high osmolality (osmosis)
- Normal serum osmolality: 280–295 mOsm/kg
- Sodium is the primary ECF osmole — changes in Na⁺ drive water movement between ICF and ECF

**Normal daily I&O (adult):**
- Intake: ~2,500 mL/day (1,200 mL oral fluids, 1,000 mL food water, 300 mL metabolic water)
- Output: ~2,500 mL/day (urine 1,200–1,500 mL, stool 200 mL, insensible 800–1,000 mL skin/lungs)
- Minimum urine output: 0.5 mL/kg/hr (adult)

sections.heading: IV Fluid Types and Clinical Indications
sections.body:
